1.blurb: Around 1800 BCE, while the Middle Kingdom was producing the Story of Sinuhe and the Instructions of Amenemhat, Babylonian scribes were composing the most complete version of the Epic of Gilgamesh — the world's oldest surviving long-form narrative. Both civilizations were producing sophisticated literary fiction at roughly the same time, independently.

The Mature Harappan civilization was roughly contemporary with the Middle Kingdom, but its writing system remains undeciphered. While Egypt was producing a literary golden age, the Indus Valley's cities were thriving with standardized weights, planned streets, and sophisticated drainage — but whatever they wrote has never been read.

The kingdom of Kerma at the Third Cataract was growing into a sophisticated state during the Middle Kingdom — with its own monumental architecture, elaborate burial customs, and long-distance trade. Senusret III's chain of frontier fortresses was a direct response to Kerma's rising power.
The New Kingdom is Egypt as an imperial superpower — a peer of the great Mesopotamian empires in the first true age of international diplomacy.
New Kingdom power rested on conquered Nubian gold — the southern colony that funded the empire and would one day rule Egypt in turn.
By now the Indus was long gone; Egypt's imperial age belongs to the Bronze Age world the Indus never reached.

2.blurb: While the Hyksos were introducing chariots to Egypt around 1650 BCE, the Hittites in Anatolia were building their own chariot-based military state. Both powers adopted the same Central Asian technology and transformed it into an empire-building weapon system.

Chariots reached Egypt via the Hyksos around 1650 BCE. They would arrive in China roughly 400 years later, around 1200 BCE, probably transmitted across the Eurasian steppe. The same military technology that transformed Near Eastern warfare eventually reached the other end of the continent.

When the Middle Kingdom collapsed and the Hyksos seized the Delta, the Kerma kingdom exploited the power vacuum to expand northward, absorbing the abandoned Egyptian frontier fortresses. The Hyksos and Kerma even coordinated against Thebes, squeezing the last independent Egyptian rulers from both directions.
The Hyksos shock that ended the Middle Kingdom brought the chariot — the same military revolution remaking Hatti and the whole Bronze Age Near East.
The war-chariot that the Hyksos forced on Egypt was, in the same centuries, transforming warfare as far as Shang China.
Egypt's expulsion of the Hyksos came with renewed conquest of Nubia — liberation in the north, subjugation in the south.

3.blurb: Thutmose I pushed the Egyptian frontier past the Second Cataract to the Fourth Cataract — further into Africa than any pharaoh had gone. He appointed the first Viceroy of Kush, beginning centuries of Egyptian colonial rule over Nubia that would profoundly shape Kushite civilization.

As Thutmose I was reaching the Euphrates and establishing Egypt as a Near Eastern superpower in the 1490s BCE, the Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating power in Anatolia. The two empires were on a collision course that would climax at Kadesh two centuries later.
Liberated, imperial Egypt now collided with the other Bronze Age great powers — above all the rising Hittite Empire in Anatolia.

5.blurb: While Thutmose III was building the largest Egyptian empire in history, the Hittites in Anatolia were rebuilding after their own civil wars. Within a century the two empires would be locked in competition for control of Syria — a rivalry that climaxed at the Battle of Kadesh.

Thutmose III built a temple at Jebel Barkal, a sacred mountain near the Fourth Cataract. Centuries later, the Kushite kings would make Jebel Barkal the spiritual heart of their own kingdom, adopting the Egyptian religious traditions that the New Kingdom pharaohs had planted there.

6.blurb: The Amarna Letters show the Hittite king cautiously approaching Egypt for diplomatic contact during Amenhotep III's reign. Within a generation, the Hittites under Suppiluliuma I would become Egypt's most dangerous rival, exploiting the Amarna crisis to seize Egyptian vassal states in Syria.

While Amenhotep III was exchanging gold and princesses with Babylon and Mitanni, the Shang Dynasty was at its height 5,000 miles east — producing the finest bronze vessels in the world and practicing oracle-bone divination at Yinxu. Two great Bronze Age civilizations at their peak, with no knowledge of each other.

Mycenaean pottery found in Egyptian contexts from Amenhotep III's reign confirms indirect trade contacts between the Egyptian and Aegean worlds. The Mycenaean palaces were importing Egyptian luxury goods — gold, faience, glass — while exporting their distinctive stirrup jars across the Mediterranean.

While Amenhotep III was building Luxor Temple and exchanging diplomatic letters with Babylon, the Initial Period temple-builders of coastal Peru were constructing massive U-shaped ceremonial complexes — an ocean and a world away from the Bronze Age diplomatic circuit.
Amenhotep III's golden age is the Amarna-letters world — Egypt, Hatti, Babylon, Mitanni as a club of great kings exchanging gold and brides.
Mycenaean Greece was a junior player in this same diplomatic-trade system — pottery and envoys moving between Thebes, Hattusa, and Mycenae.
While Egypt ran Bronze Age superpower diplomacy, the Andes were on a wholly separate track — a reminder the 'world system' was only half the world.

7.blurb: While Akhenaten was absorbed in his religious revolution, the Hittite king Suppiluliuma I was systematically dismantling Egypt's Levantine vassal network. The Amarna Letters preserve desperate pleas from Egyptian allies that went unanswered as the court focused on Atenism instead of empire.

8.blurb: The Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE was the climax of the Egyptian-Hittite rivalry — the largest chariot battle in recorded history, fought for control of Syria. Both sides claimed victory; the reality was a stalemate that exhausted both empires and eventually led to the world's oldest peace treaty.

Hattusili III came to the Hittite throne through a coup against his nephew. The peace treaty he signed with Ramesses II in 1259 BCE ended decades of war — both empires recognized they needed each other more than they needed Syria. The alliance held until the Hittite Empire was destroyed by the Bronze Age Collapse.
Ramesses II's reign is defined by Kadesh and the world's first surviving peace treaty — with the Hittite Empire, the other Bronze Age superpower.

9.blurb: The Hittite Empire was one of the first casualties of the Bronze Age Collapse — Hattusha was burned and abandoned around 1180 BCE. Egypt's treaty partner of fifty years simply ceased to exist, taking with it half the international diplomatic system that had kept the Near East stable.

The Mycenaean palaces of Greece were destroyed or abandoned during the same Bronze Age Collapse that devastated the Hittites and Sea Peoples. The palatial civilization that had traded with Egypt and controlled the Aegean vanished, plunging Greece into a Dark Age that lasted centuries.

9.matchText: kings of Kush who conquered Egypt
9.blurb: The fragmentation that followed the New Kingdom's collapse eventually led to the Kushite conquest of Egypt — the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty of Nubian pharaohs who ruled the same country that had once colonized their ancestors. Five centuries of Egyptian cultural colonialism in Nubia had produced kings who felt entitled to rule Egypt itself.
The same catastrophe that battered Ramesses III erased Mycenaean Greece — one systemic collapse across the whole eastern Mediterranean.
